behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in validation logic and sequencing of validation before require(). We can mock the filesystem (return invalid manifest) and mock require() to track whether it's called. If the loader has the bug of loading before validating, our mock will detect the require() call and fail the test. No real I/O or module execution needed — the ordering logic can be fully tested in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock filesystem returning a manifest missing a required field (e.g., no 'name'). Mock require() to throw if called. Call the loader and verify: (1) validation rejects the plugin and (2) require() is never invoked for that plugin.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: This is a dependency-ordering algorithm that can be tested purely in-process. Mock the manifests (A→B dependency), mock the require/module system, and instrument the onLoad hooks to track call order. A bug in the dependency resolver (calling A before B) will be caught when B.onLoad is not called first. No real module loading needed — the ordering logic is independent of actual plugin code.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugins where A depends on B. Mock each plugin's onLoad hook to record when it's called. Load both plugins and verify that B.onLoad() is invoked before A.onLoad().

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is a pure graph-algorithm problem. Mock the manifests with circular dependencies (A→B, B→A) and verify the loader detects the cycle and rejects both. This is straightforward to test without any real module loading — the failure is in the graph traversal logic, which is deterministic and in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugin manifests where each depends on the other. Call the loader and verify: (1) a cycle error is reported and (2) neither plugin's onLoad hook is called.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is: what if the module's top-level initialization code hasn't run when onLoad is called? For example, if the plugin sets up global state or a database connection during module load, and onLoad assumes that state exists, a bug here manifests as subtle state initialization failures. To catch this reliably, we must actually import real plugin modules and verify their initialization side effects have occurred before onLoad is invoked. Mocking require() alone is insufficient because it doesn't execute the module's initialization code.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real test plugin module that sets a flag or state during top-level execution (e.g., module.exports.initialized = true). Have the plugin's onLoad hook check that this flag is set. If the loader calls onLoad before importing the module, the flag will be undefined and onLoad will fail. Load the plugin and verify it initializes successfully.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: Error resilience and selective continuation is testable with pure in-process logic. Mock three plugins (A throws, B depends on A, C does not depend on A). Mock C's onLoad to verify it's called despite A's failure. A bug in the error handling (e.g., stopping all initialization when one fails) will be caught. No real module loading or exception propagation across processes needed — the error handling logic is synchronous and local.
behaviors.test_description: Create three mock plugins: A (onLoad throws), B (depends on A), C (no dependency on A). Mock their onLoad hooks to track invocation. Call the loader and verify: (1) A's error is caught/reported, (2) B.onLoad is never called (depends on failed plugin), (3) C.onLoad is called (no dependency on A).
